input: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I'd say that, on a causal level, it's possible to argue that Billy and Suzy both contributed to triggering the motion detector. However, if we delve deeper into the concept of causality and moral responsibility, I'd like to consider whether their actions were intentional or not. The instruction from their boss was clear: 'Be sure you are here at exactly 9 am.' It's likely that they both understood the significance of arriving precisely at 9 am, and their actions in doing so can be seen as fulfilling that intention. If we assign moral responsibility for triggering the motion detector to either Billy or Suzy, I'd argue that it's more accurate to say they unintentionally caused the detection to occur. Their primary goal was to complete their task on time, not to trigger a security system.

[Legal_Expert]: I appreciate your nuanced approach, Ethical_Philosopher, but from a purely legal perspective, this scenario raises interesting questions about duty of care and negligence. The fact that Billy and Suzy followed the boss's explicit instructions to arrive at exactly 9 am suggests they took reasonable care in complying with their employer's requirements. Triggering the motion detector was an unforeseen consequence of their actions, rather than a deliberate intent to cause harm or trigger the security system. In many jurisdictions, strict liability for triggering a security system might not apply if the individual had taken all necessary precautions and followed instructions from their employer.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think I'd want to assign some blame to Billy. If Suzy arrives first and Billy follows her exactly 9 minutes later, it's hard not to interpret that as a deliberate action on his part. He knew he was right behind Suzy, so when the motion detector went off, it seems like an unlikely coincidence that it happened after Suzy arrived.

[Legal_Expert]: That's a fair point, Ordinary_Citizen, but I'd counter by saying that Billy's knowledge of Suzy's arrival time doesn't necessarily imply he intended to cause the motion detector to go off. If they had synchronized their arrival times, it would be a different story. However, since they did arrive at 9 am in close succession, it's possible that Suzy's arrival triggered the motion detector, and Billy's follow-up action was simply an unfortunate consequence of the situation unfolding as planned.

[Causal_Scientist]: I think Ordinary_Citizen has a point worth exploring further. The idea that Billy's knowledge of Suzy's arrival time implies he intentionally caused the motion detector to go off is a bit of a slippery slope. If we start assigning moral responsibility based on proximity or timing, it could lead to absurd conclusions. Consider this: what if Suzy had been late and Billy had followed her anyway? Would that mean Billy was trying to cause trouble? In that case, I think the notion of unintentional causality becomes even more complicated.
